                                                Current Population Survey


          The State and regional labor force data            tion of the State's population residing in primary
      presented in this release were developed by            sampling units (PSU's) which have been desig-
      the Bureau of Labor Statistics from information        nated as self-rep resenting areas (i.e., PSU's
      collected as part of the Current Population            which come into the sample with statistical
      Survey (CPS), a national sample survey of 50,000       certainty). 1/ States having a relatively high
      households conducted monthly by the Bureau of          proportion of their population residing in these
      the Census for the Bureau of Labor Statistics.         PSU's consequently will have more accurate
      The CPS is explained fully in Concepts and             labor force statistics. Most of the States dis-
      Methods Used in Manpower Statistics from the           cussed in this release fit into this classification.
      Current Population Surveys BLS Report 313,
      which is available from the Bureau of Labor             Tables of Standard Error for 10 States
      Statistics upon request.

            Illustrative use of standard error tables. To          in Pennsylvania, refer to table F. Linear inter-
        find the standard error for the 390,000 estimate           polation using the sixth and seventh rows of the
        of total unemployment in California, for example,          table along with the second and third columns
        refer to table C. Linear interpolation in the first        shows that the standard error of the unemploy-
        row of the table shows that the standard error             ment rate is about .2. Thus, the chances are
        of the unemployment estimate is about 12,000.               about 68 out of 100 that the actual unemploy-
        Consequently, the chances are about 68 out of              ment rate of white persons in Pennsylvania
        100 that the sample estimate would differ by               falls between 2.8 and 3.2 percent; the chances
        less than 12,000 from a figure which would                 are about 90 out of 100 that the rate falls
        have been obtained from a complete count of                between 2.7 and 3.3 percent.
        unemployed persons in California. For greater
        statistical reliability (i.e., 90 chances out of
        100), standard error figures should be multi-                  Tables of Standard Error for Regions
        plied by 1.6. At this higher level of statistical
        accuracy, the standard error of the California    The statistical variability of the regional

                                                 Insured jobless under State unemployment insurance programs excludes workers
                                                 who have exhausted their benefit rights, new workers, and persons from jobs
                                                 not covered by State unemployment insurance programs.

Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis
                                              ANNOUNCEMENT

            The February and March 1969 issues of Employment and Earnings and Monthly Report on
      the Labor Force presented 286 different historical seasonally adjusted series of labor force sta-
      tistics. Most of these data covered persons 16 years and over for the entire period and reflect
      the change in the age cutoff from 14 years and over to 16 years and over instituted in January 1937.
      However, it was not possible to revise some of series for periods prior to 1966, and therefore
      these series cover persons 14 and over until 1966 and persons 16 years and over after that point.
      The difference in most cases are very small. (See the article, "New Definitions for Employment
      and Unemployment," which appears in the February 1967 issue of Employment and Earnings and
      Monthly Report on the Labor Force; reprints of this article are available upon request.)

      February issue

      Total at work in Nonagricultural Industries—page 42 (corrected on page 122 of March issue)

                                                    Technical Note

                                         Additional information concerning the preparation
                                         of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                         and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                         survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                         technical notes for each of these series, available
                                         from the Bureau of Labor Statistics free of charge.

     ployment insurance program. It includes some persons              ing a period of unemployment. A claimant who continues

     ployed in the payroll and household surveys. Excluded             insured unemployment figure.
     are persons who have exhausted their benefit rights, new
     workers who have not earned rights to unemployment                    Because of differences in State laws and procedures
     insurance, and persons losing jobs not covered by unem-           under which unemployment insurance programs are op-
     ployment insurance systems (agriculture, State and local          erated, State unemployment rates generally indicate, but
     government, domestic service, self-employment, unpaid             do not precisely measure, differences among the indi-

                                                          ATTENTION
            As discussed in the Technical Note, the Bureau periodically adjusts the industry employ-
      ment series to a recent benchmark to improve their accuracy. These adjustments may also
      affect the hours and earnings series because employment levels are used as weights. Industry
      data for all national series shown in this report have been adjusted to March 1967 benchmarks.
      Data from April 1967 forward are subject to revision at the time of the next benchmark.

           Beginning with the June 1968 and subsequent issues of Employment and Earnings and
      Monthly Report on the Labor Force, the national data in sections B, C, and D supersede
      those published in previous issues, as well as those appearing in the Handbook of Labor Statis-
      tics, 1968. Comparable data are published in Employment and Earnings Statistics for the
      United States, 1909-68, BLS Bulletin 1312-6.
